query: how to fix a leaky faucet

text_blocks.answer: Fixing a leaky faucet is a simple DIY project that usually just requires replacing worn rubber washers, O-rings, or the internal cartridge. First, shut off the water valves beneath the sink. Remove the handle, pull out the faulty part, and buy an exact match at your local hardware store.
1. Shut Off the Water and Drain Lines
Locate the water supply valves under the sink and turn them clockwise to shut them off

Find and remove the decorative cap on the top or side of the faucet handle using a flathead screwdriver or your fingernail. Use a screwdriver or an Allen key to unscrew the handle, then lift it straight off.
Use an adjustable wrench to unscrew the packing nut (or bonnet nut) that secures the cartridge or valve stem. Gently pull the cartridge straight out.

Take the removed cartridge, rubber washers, and springs to a hardware store to find an exact replacement. Alternatively, you can search for replacement parts using your faucet's brand (e.g., Delta, Moen, Kohler) and model number. Once you have the new parts, drop the new springs and washers into place, and gently press the new cartridge or stem into the housing.

Screw the packing nut back on. Be careful not to overtighten it, or you may crack delicate pieces. Reattach the handle, screw it in, and snap the decorative cap back on. Slowly turn your water supply valves back on. Remove the aerator (the tip of the faucet spout) and run the water for a few minutes to flush out any debris, then screw the aerator back on.

text_blocks.snippet: Fixing a leaky faucet is a simple DIY project that usually just requires replacing worn rubber washers, O-rings, or the internal cartridge. First, shut off the water valves beneath the sink. Remove the handle, pull out the faulty part, and buy an exact match at your local hardware store.

Needle-nose pliers or a pick tool
Replacement parts (O-rings, washers, springs, or a new cartridge)

text_blocks.snippet: 1. Shut Off the Water and Drain Lines

Locate the water supply valves under the sink and turn them clockwise to shut them off. Open the faucet handles to let any remaining water drain out. Pro tip: Place a towel or tape over the sink drain to prevent small screws from falling down the pipe. YouTube·doityourselfstan +3

Find and remove the decorative cap on the top or side of the faucet handle using a flathead screwdriver or your fingernail. Use a screwdriver or an Allen key to unscrew the handle, then lift it straight off.
Use an adjustable wrench to unscrew the packing nut (or bonnet nut) that secures the cartridge or valve stem. Gently pull the cartridge straight out.

list.snippet: For compression or two-handle faucets: Look inside the cavity to locate the small rubber washer and spring. Use needle-nose pliers or a pick tool to pull them out.
For single-handle faucets: The issue is usually a degraded cartridge or worn O-rings.

text_blocks.snippet: Take the removed cartridge, rubber washers, and springs to a hardware store to find an exact replacement. Alternatively, you can search for replacement parts using your faucet's brand (e.g., Delta, Moen, Kohler) and model number. Once you have the new parts, drop the new springs and washers into place, and gently press the new cartridge or stem into the housing.

Screw the packing nut back on. Be careful not to overtighten it, or you may crack delicate pieces. Reattach the handle, screw it in, and snap the decorative cap back on. Slowly turn your water supply valves back on. Remove the aerator (the tip of the faucet spout) and run the water for a few minutes to flush out any debris, then screw the aerator back on.

extracted.summary:
Fixing a leaky faucet is a simple DIY project that usually just requires `replacing worn rubber washers, O-rings, or the internal cartridge` . First, shut off the water valves beneath the sink. Remove the handle, pull out the faulty part, and buy an exact match at your local hardware store.[](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lW4fFBY6g_s&vl=en&t=497) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lW4fFBY6g_s&vl=en&t=497)[[2]](https://www.aeroenergy.com/an-experts-guide-to-leaky-faucet-repair/)[[3]](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/taB8Jl47ets)[[4]](https://www.homedepot.com/c/ah/how-to-fix-a-leaky-faucet/9ba683603be9fa5395fab90ee6659fb)

Tools You Will Need:

- Adjustable wrench or channel locks
- Screwdrivers (Flathead and Phillips) or Allen keys
- Needle-nose pliers or a pick tool
- Replacement parts (O-rings, washers, springs, or a new cartridge)[](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rcV6CwoKwGg) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rcV6CwoKwGg)[[2]](https://www.homedepot.com/c/ah/how-to-fix-a-leaky-faucet/9ba683603be9fa5395fab90ee6659fb)[[3]](https://www.aeroenergy.com/an-experts-guide-to-leaky-faucet-repair/)[[4]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lW4fFBY6g_s&vl=en&t=497)[[5]](https://www.instagram.com/reel/DR77MXOD-AG/)


---

Step-by-Step Guide

1. Shut Off the Water and Drain Lines

Locate the water supply valves under the sink and turn them clockwise to shut them off . Open the faucet handles to let any remaining water drain out. *Pro tip: Place a towel or tape over the sink drain to prevent small screws from falling down the pipe.* [](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=f_uADyhDDpE&t=14) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=f_uADyhDDpE&t=14)[[2]](https://www.aeroenergy.com/an-experts-guide-to-leaky-faucet-repair/)[[3]](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/taB8Jl47ets)[[4]](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/RUX3U6CIRXI)

2. Remove the Handle Find and remove the decorative cap on the top or side of the faucet handle using a flathead screwdriver or your fingernail. Use a screwdriver or an Allen key to unscrew the handle, then lift it straight off.[](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rcV6CwoKwGg) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rcV6CwoKwGg)[[2]](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/taB8Jl47ets)[[3]](https://www.clackamasproviders.org/fix-a-leaky-faucet/)[[4]](https://www.deltafaucet.com/il/home-improvement-101/diy-easiest-way-fix-leaky-faucet)3. Access the Internal Components Use an adjustable wrench to unscrew the packing nut (or bonnet nut) that secures the cartridge or valve stem. Gently pull the cartridge straight out.[](https://www.homedepot.com/c/ah/how-to-fix-a-leaky-faucet/9ba683603be9fa5395fab90ee6659fb) [[1]](https://www.homedepot.com/c/ah/how-to-fix-a-leaky-faucet/9ba683603be9fa5395fab90ee6659fb)[[2]](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/taB8Jl47ets)

- **For compression or two-handle faucets:** Look inside the cavity to locate the small rubber washer and spring. Use needle-nose pliers or a pick tool to pull them out.
- **For single-handle faucets:** The issue is usually a degraded cartridge or worn O-rings.[](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rcV6CwoKwGg) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rcV6CwoKwGg)[[2]](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/RUX3U6CIRXI)[[3]](https://www.edsheatingandcooling.com/blog/how-to-detect-and-fix-a-leaky-faucet/)[[4]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lW4fFBY6g_s&vl=en&t=497)

4. Replace the Worn Parts Take the removed cartridge, rubber washers, and springs to a hardware store to find an exact replacement. Alternatively, you can search for replacement parts using your faucet's brand (e.g., Delta, Moen, Kohler) and model number. Once you have the new parts, drop the new springs and washers into place, and gently press the new cartridge or stem into the housing.[](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/d1IC7x-g1I4) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/d1IC7x-g1I4)[[2]](https://www.homedepot.com/c/ah/how-to-fix-a-leaky-faucet/9ba683603be9fa5395fab90ee6659fb)[[3]](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/taB8Jl47ets)[[4]](https://www.mauicounty.gov/241/Fix-A-Leaky-Faucet-By-Yourself)[[5]](https://www.armadillo.one/resources/how-to-fix-a-kitchen-faucet-and-stop-the-drip/)5. Reassemble and Test Screw the packing nut back on. Be careful not to overtighten it, or you may crack delicate pieces. Reattach the handle, screw it in, and snap the decorative cap back on. Slowly turn your water supply valves back on. Remove the aerator (the tip of the faucet spout) and run the water for a few minutes to flush out any debris, then screw the aerator back on.[](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rcV6CwoKwGg) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rcV6CwoKwGg)[[2]](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/taB8Jl47ets)[[3]](https://www.aeroenergy.com/an-experts-guide-to-leaky-faucet-repair/)[[4]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lW4fFBY6g_s&vl=en&t=497)[[5]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DX-OLwqFdHI)[[6]](https://www.homedepot.com/c/ah/how-to-fix-a-leaky-faucet/9ba683603be9fa5395fab90ee6659fb)
---

For a visual breakdown of how to identify the right replacement parts and work with tapered springs, this video guide is highly recommended:![Related video thumbnail](https://encrypted-tbn2.gstatic.com/images?q=tbn:ANd9GcTqH-q3CeFwTnfOMnCV5313JS2tH3b5ZElVMXrejIu6gOL72N1S)

Step-by-Step Guide

Locate the water supply valves under the sink and turn them clockwise to shut them off . Open the faucet handles to let any remaining water drain out. *Pro tip: Place a towel or tape over the sink drain to prevent small screws from falling down the pipe.* [](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=f_uADyhDDpE&t=14) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=f_uADyhDDpE&t=14)[[2]](https://www.aeroenergy.com/an-experts-guide-to-leaky-faucet-repair/)[[3]](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/taB8Jl47ets)[[4]](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/RUX3U6CIRXI)

Access the Internal Components Use an adjustable wrench to unscrew the packing nut (or bonnet nut) that secures the cartridge or valve stem. Gently pull the cartridge straight out.[](https://www.homedepot.com/c/ah/how-to-fix-a-leaky-faucet/9ba683603be9fa5395fab90ee6659fb) [[1]](https://www.homedepot.com/c/ah/how-to-fix-a-leaky-faucet/9ba683603be9fa5395fab90ee6659fb)[[2]](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/taB8Jl47ets)

- **For compression or two-handle faucets:** Look inside the cavity to locate the small rubber washer and spring. Use needle-nose pliers or a pick tool to pull them out.
- **For single-handle faucets:** The issue is usually a degraded cartridge or worn O-rings.[](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rcV6CwoKwGg) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rcV6CwoKwGg)[[2]](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/RUX3U6CIRXI)[[3]](https://www.edsheatingandcooling.com/blog/how-to-detect-and-fix-a-leaky-faucet/)[[4]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lW4fFBY6g_s&vl=en&t=497)

4. Replace the Worn Parts Take the removed cartridge, rubber washers, and springs to a hardware store to find an exact replacement. Alternatively, you can search for replacement parts using your faucet's brand (e.g., Delta, Moen, Kohler) and model number. Once you have the new parts, drop the new springs and washers into place, and gently press the new cartridge or stem into the housing.[](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/d1IC7x-g1I4) [[1]](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/d1IC7x-g1I4)[[2]](https://www.homedepot.com/c/ah/how-to-fix-a-leaky-faucet/9ba683603be9fa5395fab90ee6659fb)[[3]](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/taB8Jl47ets)[[4]](https://www.mauicounty.gov/241/Fix-A-Leaky-Faucet-By-Yourself)[[5]](https://www.armadillo.one/resources/how-to-fix-a-kitchen-faucet-and-stop-the-drip/)5.
